revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]][[Category:Unsorted revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]][[Category:Unsorted {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] [[Category:Completion {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] [[Category:Completion revision.comment: Text replacement - "天児屋根命}}" to "天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] revision.comment: Text replacement - "|天武天皇}}" to "|天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu}}"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|天武天皇|1=Emperor Tenmu}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] revision.comment: Text replacement - "天武天皇" to "Emperor Tenmu"
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]] revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name = Munakata Shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), haiden.jpg|280px]]
Worship Hall |location = [[Nara Prefecture]] [[Sakurai City]], Toyama 818 |coordinates = {{Coord|34|30|39.0|N|135|51|35.0|E|region:JP-29_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} |Deities = {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}}
{{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}}
{{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} |Rank = {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha, 3 shrines)
Former {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} |Establishment = Before the {{ill|Reign of Emperor Tenmu|ja|Emperor Tenmu|1=Emperor Tenmu}} |Main Hall = {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}}, copper-roofed |Alias = |Temple Affiliation = |festival = October 19 |Rituals = |Map = {{location map|Japan Nara Prefecture|label=Munakata Shrine|float=center|relief=1|width=256}} }} [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), torii.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Torii}}]] '''Munakata Shrine''' (宗像神社) is a [[Shinto shrine]] located in Toyama, [[Sakurai City]], [[Nara Prefecture]]. It is an {{ill|Shikinaisha|ja|式内社}} (Myojin Taisha) under the {{ill|Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines|ja|近代社格制度|1=Modern system of ranked Shinto shrines}}, previously classified as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}}. The shrine is situated at the foot of {{ill|Torimiyama|ja|鳥見山}}. == Shrine name == From the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}}, the shrine was referred to as "Kasuga Shrine" but reverted to its original name in {{ill|Meiji 8|ja|明治8年}} (1875). In later periods, it was also known locally as "Nakajima Shrine" or "Nakajima-san." == Enshrined Deities == The shrine enshrines three deities, collectively known as the {{ill|Munakata Three Goddesses|ja|宗像三女神|1=Three Munakata Goddesses}}: * {{ill|Tagirihime|ja|多紀理毘売命|1=Tagorihime}} * {{ill|Ichikishimahime|ja|市寸嶋比売命}} * {{ill|Tagitsuhime|ja|田寸津比売命}} == History == The shrine's foundation is traditionally attributed to the influence of {{ill|Munakata no Tokuyoshi|ja|宗形徳善}}, whose daughter, Amako, became a consort of Emperor Tenmu and the mother of {{ill|Prince Takai|ja|高市皇子}}. It is believed the shrine was established as a family deity of Prince Takai's maternal lineage, though some sources suggest that the Munakata clan enshrined the three goddesses here earlier.Refer to the detailed studies of the Munakata deity origins in Yamato. The shrine was later associated with the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}}, descendants of Prince Takai, who revered it as their clan shrine. In {{ill|元慶|ja|元慶}} 4 (880 CE), it was recognized as a government-supported shrine (官社) according to the {{ill|Nihon Sandai Jitsuroku|ja|日本三代実録}}. In {{ill|寛平|ja|寛平}} 5 (893 CE), the shrine received provisions for repair and continued to be closely tied to the Takai lineage.See "Taikami and Regional Shinto Practices." The shrine declined during the {{ill|Nanboku-chō period|ja|南北朝時代}} due to conflicts but was later restored in {{ill|Tensho|ja|天正}} 18 (1590 CE) by the Takai family. Significant restoration efforts occurred in the {{ill|安政|ja|安政}} period (1854–1860 CE) and again in the {{ill|Heisei|ja|平成}} era (2009). === Divine Rank === The shrine was elevated to government-supported status in 元慶 4 and attained "Jūichi-i Kunhachi-tō" (従一位勲八等) in 元慶 5 according to historical records. == Rituals == The shrine's rituals are conducted through two traditional {{ill|Miyaza|ja|宮座}} systems: 1. "Ōtōya," based on individual parishioners 2. "Kotōya," based on households The annual festival is observed on October 19, reflecting changes from the lunar calendar to the Gregorian calendar. === Shrine Priests === From the time of Emperor Tenmu, the {{ill|Takai Clan|ja|高階氏}} served as shrine priests. Their lineage has maintained this role into modern times, adopting the name "Tamai Clan" in later generations. == Shrine Buildings == [[File:Munakata-jinja (Sakurai), honden.jpg|thumb|220px|right|{{center|Main Hall}}]] The main hall is a three-by-two-bay {{ill|Shinmei-zukuri|ja|神明造}} structure with copper roofing. It was renovated in 2009 during the Heisei period. == Sub-Shrines == * {{ill|Kasuga Shrine|ja|春日社}}: Enshrines {{ill|Takamimusubi|ja|天児屋根命|1=Ame-no-Koyane}}, {{ill|Takeminakata|ja|武甕槌命}}, {{ill|Futsunushi|ja|経津主命}}, and a "Hime deity" * Kasuga Wakamiya Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Ameoshikunune|ja|天押雲根命}} * Rokusho Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Izanagi|ja|伊邪那岐命}}, {{ill|Amaterasu|ja|天照大神}}, {{ill|Tsukuyomi|ja|月読命}}, {{ill|Izanami|ja|伊邪那美命}}, {{ill|Susanoo|ja|素盞嗚尊命}}, and {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大国主命}} * Kotohira Shrine: Enshrines {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|ja|崇徳天皇}} The grounds include a monument marking the origin of {{ill|Hōshō School|ja|宝生流}} in Noh theater. == Access == * From {{ill|JR|ja|西日本旅客鉄道}} {{ill|Sakurai Line|ja|桜井線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Osaka Line|ja|近鉄大阪線}}, disembark at {{ill|Sakurai Station|ja|桜井駅}} and take a Nara Kotsu bus to Toyama Station. == Nearby Sites == * {{ill|Sakurai Chausuyama Kofun|ja|桜井茶臼山古墳}} == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == * {{ill|Research on Shikinaisha|ja|式内社調査報告}} Vol. 3, Yamato (B), Kōgakkan University Press, Showa 57 * Edited by Kenichi Tanikawa, 'The Gods of Japan - Shrines and Sacred Sites - Volume 4: Yamato,' Hakusuisha, 1985 == External Links == {{Commonscat|Munakata-jinja (Sakurai)}} * [http://21coe.kokugakuin.ac.jp/db/jinja/kindex2.php?J_ID=24701 Munakata Shrine] - Kokugakuin University, 21st Century COE Program * [http://www.begin.or.jp/~munakata/jinjya.html Munakata Shrine] - Toyama District Community Center, "Toyama Stories" {{Shinto Horizontal}} [[Category:Munakata Shrine (Sakurai)]]
